concept.name: TCP connection lifecycle
concept.definition: The full set of steps two computers go through to talk reliably over a network: they first greet each other to agree to start, then send messages back and forth while confirming each one arrived, and finally say goodbye to close the conversation cleanly.

audience: a curious adult with no domain background
atomic_reason: The lifecycle's own mechanism is one clear picture: a greeting handshake, confirmed message passing, and a goodbye. But it leans on ideas a newcomer would not already know, like the chopped-up chunks a message travels as and the agreed rules two machines follow, so each of those deserves its own picture first.

prerequisites.name: Data packets
prerequisites.definition: Small labeled chunks that a message is chopped into before being sent across a network, each traveling on its own and carrying enough information to be put back in order.
prerequisites.why: A TCP conversation does not send a message in one piece; it ships many small chunks and confirms each one arrived, so the reader must first picture what those chunks are before the greet-confirm-goodbye steps make sense.

prerequisites.name: Communication protocol
prerequisites.definition: An agreed set of rules two computers follow when talking, so each one knows what kind of message to expect next and what every message means.
prerequisites.why: The whole lifecycle is one particular set of agreed rules, so the reader needs the general idea that machines follow a shared rulebook before seeing the specific greet-confirm-goodbye rules TCP uses.

prerequisites.name: Delivery acknowledgement
prerequisites.definition: A short reply one computer sends back to confirm it received what the other sent, so the sender knows the message arrived and does not have to guess or resend it blindly.
prerequisites.why: TCP is called reliable precisely because every chunk is answered with a confirmation, and if no confirmation comes the chunk is sent again, so the reader cannot understand why the lifecycle is trustworthy without this back-and-forth confirming idea.
